title.en: Flutter vs React Native for Screenshot to Code

h1.en: Flutter vs React Native: AI Code Generation Comparison

description.en: A detailed comparison of AI-generated Flutter Dart vs React Native JSX from screenshots — covering widget vs component models, performance, language differences, and when to choose each platform.

intro.en: Flutter and React Native are the two dominant cross-platform mobile frameworks in 2026. Both allow writing one codebase that runs on iOS and Android — but their architectures, languages, and UI models are fundamentally different. When generating code from a UI screenshot, the AI produces Dart widget trees for Flutter and JSX component trees for React Native. This comparison covers what matters for screenshot-to-code workflows.

a.en: Dart

b.en: JavaScript / TypeScript

a.en: Custom rendering engine (Impeller/Skia)

b.en: Native platform UI components via bridge

a.en: Widget tree (StatelessWidget / StatefulWidget)

b.en: React component tree (functional components)

a.en: Widget properties and decoration

b.en: StyleSheet.create() or NativeWind (Tailwind)

a.en: Yes (very fast)

b.en: Yes (fast with Metro bundler)

a.en: Custom (identical cross-platform)

b.en: Native platform components (slightly different per OS)

a.en: Yes (Flutter Web)

b.en: Yes (React Native Web / Expo)

a.en: High (compiles to native ARM)

b.en: Good (New Architecture with JSI)

a.en: pub.dev — growing fast

b.en: npm — vast, web-portable packages

a.en: Pixel-perfect custom rendering looks identical on iOS and Android
Dart compiles to native ARM code for high performance
Excellent for pixel-precise custom designs
Single codebase covers mobile, web, and desktop
Strong Material Design and Cupertino widget libraries
Google backing and strong enterprise adoption

b.en: JavaScript knowledge transfers from web development
Uses native platform UI components for authentic OS feel
Vast npm ecosystem including web-compatible packages
Expo provides zero-config setup for rapid prototyping
React knowledge transfers — same component patterns as React
Large community with long track record

a.en: Dart is a niche language with smaller talent pool
Custom rendering means UI may not look exactly like native apps
Larger app binary sizes compared to React Native
pub.dev package ecosystem smaller than npm

b.en: JavaScript bridge can cause performance bottlenecks (Old Architecture)
Native component behavior differences between iOS and Android require platform-specific code
More complex dependency management than Flutter
Metro bundler is slower than Flutter's hot reload for large projects

a.en: Use Flutter (Screenshot to Flutter) when: you need pixel-perfect custom designs that look identical on iOS and Android; you're building a new app without existing JavaScript codebase; performance and smooth animations are critical; or you need desktop and web support from the same codebase. Flutter excels for design-heavy, high-performance mobile apps.

b.en: Use React Native (Screenshot to React Native) when: your team already knows React and JavaScript; you're reusing web components in a mobile app; you want native platform look-and-feel on each OS; or you're using Expo for rapid prototyping. React Native is ideal for teams with web development backgrounds building mobile apps.

performanceNote.en: Flutter's Impeller rendering engine (Flutter 3.10+) delivers consistently high frame rates through direct GPU rendering, independent of the platform's UI toolkit. React Native's New Architecture (JSI + Fabric) dramatically improved bridge performance, closing the gap with Flutter. For most business app use cases, both deliver smooth 60fps UI. For graphic-intensive apps (games, complex animations), Flutter holds an edge.

a.en: iOS, Android, Web (Flutter Web), macOS, Windows, Linux

b.en: iOS, Android, Web (React Native Web / Expo), macOS (Mac Catalyst)

verdict.en: For AI screenshot-to-code workflows, choose based on your team's language expertise. If your team knows JavaScript and React, Screenshot to React Native produces familiar, immediately usable code. If you're committed to Flutter or starting fresh without a JavaScript codebase, Screenshot to Flutter generates idiomatic Dart that drops directly into your Flutter project. Both tools produce high-quality output — the decision is about your existing skill set, not the AI tool's capability.

question.en: Is Flutter or React Native better for AI code generation accuracy?

answer.en: Flutter output tends to be more structurally consistent because Flutter's widget model is explicit — every layout decision maps to a named widget. React Native's StyleSheet system (similar to CSS) gives the AI more flexibility, sometimes resulting in more visually accurate output for complex custom designs. Overall accuracy is similar; structural clarity is higher in Flutter output.

question.en: Can I preview Flutter output without a Flutter setup?

answer.en: DropAnyConvert provides a live code preview panel for the generated output. For running the code in an actual Flutter app, you need Flutter installed locally (flutter.dev) or use DartPad.dev for simple widgets. Full app integration requires the Flutter SDK and either an iOS simulator or Android emulator.

question.en: What is the learning curve difference for new developers?

answer.en: For developers with web experience, React Native has a lower learning curve because it uses JavaScript/TypeScript and React patterns already familiar from web development. Flutter requires learning Dart, which is a new language for most developers. However, Dart is designed to be learnable quickly for developers who know Java, Swift, or TypeScript — most developers become productive in Flutter within a few weeks.

question.en: Can the AI generate code for Expo (React Native)?
question.es: Can the AI generate code for Expo (React Native)?
question.fr: Can the AI generate code for Expo (React Native)?
question.de: Can the AI generate code for Expo (React Native)?
question.pt: Can the AI generate code for Expo (React Native)?

answer.en: Yes. Screenshot to React Native generates Expo-compatible code by default — it uses cross-platform React Native components (View, Text, Image, TouchableOpacity) that work in both bare React Native and Expo managed workflow projects. Expo-specific APIs (like Expo Router navigation) are noted as comments when detected from the screenshot.
